parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- creates a sense of thorough comprehension — 'standing through' something completely. It's less about going astray (as in versprechen) and more about full absorption or penetration of meaning.
From stehen (to stand). The same root appears in bestehen (to exist/pass), entstehen (to arise), and Verständnis (understanding).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.exampleSentence: Ich verstehe nicht, was du meinst.
words.exampleTranslation: I don't understand what you mean.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here er- suggests bringing something into a clear, finished state — making something fully clear to someone else.
From klar (clear). The same root appears in klären (to clarify), Klarheit (clarity), and aufklären (to enlighten/inform).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to explain, to declare, to clarify
words.exampleSentence: Kannst du mir das bitte erklären?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you please explain that to me?

parts.homeLangDetails: Here be- turns the intransitive concept of 'seeking' into a directed, transitive action — you're actively going to/toward someone or somewhere.
From suchen (to seek, to look for). The idea is of seeking out a person or place. The same root appears in suchen, versuchen (to try), and aufsuchen (to seek out).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to visit, to attend, to call on
words.exampleSentence: Wir besuchen morgen meine Großeltern.
words.exampleTranslation: We are visiting my grandparents tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ent- conveys separation or resolution — cutting away uncertainty or ambiguity. It gives the word a sense of decisive severance, like slicing through indecision.
From scheiden (to separate, to divide, to part). The same root appears in Unterschied (difference), Scheidung (divorce), and bescheiden (modest). The core idea is of cutting or drawing a line.
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to decide, to determine, to resolve
words.exampleSentence: Du musst dich jetzt entscheiden.
words.exampleTranslation: You have to decide now.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- suggests transformation or thorough processing — working something through until it's changed into something else, whether material or emotional.
From Arbeit (work, labor). The same root appears in arbeiten (to work), Arbeiter (worker), and ausarbeiten (to work out/develop).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to process, to work through, to handle
words.exampleSentence: Er braucht Zeit, um das Erlebnis zu verarbeiten.
words.exampleTranslation: He needs time to process the experience.

parts.homeLangDetails: Together or combined into one. Zusammenarbeiten (to work together), Zusammenhang (connection, literally a hanging-together of things).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to cooperate, to collaborate, to work together
words.exampleSentence: Wir müssen besser zusammenarbeiten.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to work together better.

parts.homeLangDetails: Suggests upward movement, opening, or the start of something. Aufstehen (to stand up), aufmachen (to open), aufhoeren (to stop -- literally to hear something to the very end).
From machen (to make, to do). In combination with auf-, it specifically means to open something — 'make it open.' The same root is everywhere in German: machen, Maschine, and many phrasal verbs.
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to open, to open up, to undo
words.exampleSentence: Kannst du bitte die Tür aufmachen?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you please open the door?

parts.homeLangDetails: Points away, downward, or signals separation and completion. Abfahren (to depart), abgeben (to hand over), abschneiden (to cut off).
From nehmen (to take). The same root appears in annehmen (to accept), aufnehmen (to record/take up), and zunehmen (to increase/gain weight) — the antonym of abnehmen in the weight sense.
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationShort: to lose weight; to take off
words.translationLong: to take off, to remove, to decrease, to lose weight
words.exampleSentence: Sie möchte ein paar Kilo abnehmen.
words.exampleTranslation: She wants to lose a few kilos.

parts.homeLangDetails: Points toward something or signals the beginning of contact. Anrufen (to call), ankommen (to arrive), anfangen (to begin, literally to grab on to something).
From rufen (to call, to shout). The same root appears in Ruf (reputation/call), aufrufen (to call up), and Ausruf (exclamation).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to call, to phone, to ring
words.exampleSentence: Ich rufe dich morgen früh an.
words.exampleTranslation: I'll call you tomorrow morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: Something happening before, in front of, or in advance. Vorbereiten (to prepare, to ready something beforehand), vorstellen (to introduce, to place before someone).

From schlagen (to strike, to beat, to hit). In vorschlagen, the idea is of putting something forward — 'striking it out in front' for consideration. The same root appears in Schlag (blow/stroke), einschlagen (to strike in), and Vorschlag (proposal).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to suggest, to propose, to recommend
words.exampleSentence: Darf ich einen Kompromiss vorschlagen?
words.exampleTranslation: May I suggest a compromise?

parts.homeLangDetails: Here nach- suggests going after or pursuing something mentally — following a thought through. It gives the verb the sense of thinking something over carefully, rather than just thinking in general.
From denken (to think). The same root appears in Gedanke (thought), umdenken (to rethink), and Denkmal (monument — literally a 'thinking mark').
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to think over, to reflect, to ponder, to contemplate
words.exampleSentence: Ich muss darüber nachdenken.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to think about that.

parts.homeLangDetails: Through, all the way, or to completion. Durchhalten (to persevere, to hold through to the end), durchlesen (to read all the way through).
From führen (to lead, to guide, to conduct). The same root appears in Führer (leader), einführen (to introduce), and Führerschein (driver's license).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to carry out, to implement, to execute, to conduct
words.exampleSentence: Das Experiment wurde erfolgreich durchgeführt.
words.exampleTranslation: The experiment was carried out successfully.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ein- suggests entering into a state — falling into sleep. It's the same directional sense as in einsteigen (to get in) or einatmen (to breathe in).
From schlafen (to sleep). The same root appears in Schlaf (sleep), verschlafen (to oversleep), and Schlafzimmer (bedroom).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to fall asleep, to go to sleep
words.exampleSentence: Das Kind ist beim Vorlesen eingeschlafen.
words.exampleTranslation: The child fell asleep during the reading.

parts.homeLangDetails: From kommen (to come). The same root appears in ankommen (to arrive), bekommen (to receive), and willkommen (welcome).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.exampleSentence: Wann kommst du zurück?
words.exampleTranslation: When are you coming back?

parts.homeLangDetails: Here um- conveys switching or changing over — stepping from one thing to another. It's the same sense as in umziehen (to move house) or umschalten (to switch channels).
From steigen (to climb, to step, to rise). The same root appears in einsteigen (to get on/board), aussteigen (to get off), and aufsteigen (to rise/ascend).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to change trains/buses, to transfer, to switch over
words.exampleSentence: Sie müssen in Frankfurt umsteigen.
words.exampleTranslation: You need to change trains in Frankfurt.

parts.homeLangDetails: Think of English "scatter" or "shatter" -- zer- takes a verb and adds the sense of breaking something into pieces or destroying it completely. It's very productive and consistent: *zerstören* (to destroy), *zerbrechen* (to break apart), *zerschneiden* (to cut up). If something gets zer-ed, it probably won't be usable afterward.
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to break apart, to shatter, to smash
words.exampleSentence: Das Glas kann leicht zerbrechen, wenn es fällt.
words.exampleTranslation: The glass can easily shatter if it falls.

parts.homeLangDetails: This one maps almost perfectly onto English "over-" in both its physical and figurative senses. You'll see it meaning physically above (*überbrücken*, to bridge over), exceeding a limit (*übertreiben*, to exaggerate, literally to over-drive), or covering something completely (*übersetzen*, to translate, to ferry across). Watch out -- it can be separable or inseparable depending on the verb.

From legen (to lay, to place). Here the sense is mental rather than physical — 'laying something over' in your mind, turning it around and examining it. The same root appears in vorlegen (to present), belegen (to document), and ablegen (to deposit).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to consider, to think over, to reflect on, to deliberate
words.exampleSentence: Ich muss mir das gut überlegen, bevor ich entscheide.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to think that over carefully before I decide.

parts.homeLangDetails: Under or below. Can suggest support from below or being subordinate. Unterstuetzen (to support, to prop up from underneath), unterstreichen (to underline).

From suchen (to search, to look for). With unter- added, the idea is of searching underneath or looking into something closely — probing beneath the surface. The same root appears in besuchen (to visit) and versuchen (to try).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to examine, to investigate, to inspect, to test
words.exampleSentence: Der Arzt untersucht den Patienten gründlich.
words.exampleTranslation: The doctor examines the patient thoroughly.

parts.homeLangDetails: Signals that something went wrong or was done incorrectly, like English mis-. Missverstaendnis (misunderstanding), misslingen (to fail or go wrong).

From verstehen (to understand), itself a compound of ver- + stehen (to stand). The idea of 'standing under/amid' something to grasp it is the same intuition behind the English word 'understand.' Here miss- tips the whole thing in the wrong direction.
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.exampleSentence: Ich glaube, du hast mich missverstanden.
words.exampleTranslation: I think you misunderstood me.

parts.homeLangDetails: Points outward, away from a source, or to full completion. Ausgehen is to go out; ausschlafen is to sleep all the way through until fully rested.
From ruhen (to rest, to repose). Aus- here suggests a fullness or completeness — resting all the way out, fully unwinding. The same root appears in Ruhe (calm, quiet) and beruhigen (to calm down).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.translationLong: to rest, to relax, to recuperate
words.exampleSentence: Nach der langen Wanderung müssen wir uns ausruhen.
words.exampleTranslation: After the long hike, we need to rest.

parts.homeLangDetails: You'll recognize this one immediately from English "-ship" -- they're actually cognates. It forms nouns describing a collective group, a shared condition, or an abstract relationship. Think *Freundschaft* (friendship), *Gesellschaft* (society, company), and *Mannschaft* (team). It always produces a feminine noun, so it's always die -schaft.

words.exampleSentence: Ihre Freundschaft besteht schon seit vielen Jahren.
words.exampleTranslation: Their friendship has existed for many years already.

parts.homeLangDetails: Itself built from mögen (to be able, to like) + -lich (-ly/-like), giving the sense of 'able-like' or 'doable.' The -keit suffix then turns this adjective into a noun. So the full chain is: mögen → möglich → Möglichkeit.
The same role as -heit, used after stems ending in -lich, -ig, or -sam. Freundlichkeit (friendliness), Moeglichkeit (possibility). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Es gibt immer eine Möglichkeit, etwas zu verbessern.
words.exampleTranslation: There is always a possibility to improve something.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, referring to a state or quality. Gesundheit (health, from gesund), Freiheit (freedom, from frei). Always feminine.

words.translationLong: health; also said as 'bless you' after a sneeze
words.exampleSentence: Gesundheit ist das wichtigste im Leben.
words.exampleTranslation: Health is the most important thing in life.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, referring to a state or quality. Gesundheit (health, from gesund), Freiheit (freedom, from frei). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Freiheit ist ein grundlegendes Menschenrecht.
words.exampleTranslation: Freedom is a fundamental human right.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, referring to a state or quality. Gesundheit (health, from gesund), Freiheit (freedom, from frei). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Die Sicherheit der Kinder hat oberste Priorität.
words.exampleTranslation: The safety of the children is the top priority.

parts.homeLangDetails: Suggests upward movement, opening, or the start of something. Aufstehen (to stand up), aufmachen (to open), aufhoeren (to stop -- literally to hear something to the very end).

From merken (to notice, to take note of). Related to the English word 'mark' — both go back to a shared Germanic root meaning to pay attention to something, to mark it in the mind. The same root appears in bemerken (to remark, to notice) and merkwürdig (remarkable, strange).
A suffix meaning inclined toward or characterized by. Langsam (slow), achtsam (mindful), gemeinsam (shared or common, together-inclined).
The same role as -heit, used after stems ending in -lich, -ig, or -sam. Freundlichkeit (friendliness), Moeglichkeit (possibility). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Die Schüler hörten mit großer Aufmerksamkeit zu.
words.exampleTranslation: The students listened with great attention.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- helps turn the root into a complete, established action — not just standing somewhere but fully grasping something. It works similarly to the intensifying sense of ver- in other words where something is brought to a definitive state.

From stehen/standen (to stand). 'Understanding' as standing firmly within something — the same metaphor as in English 'understand.' The same root underlies Verstand (intellect, mind) and verstehen (to understand).

This suffix turns verbs or adjectives into nouns, usually naming the result or condition of something. It's a bit like English "-ness" or "-ment" in feel. Common examples include *Ergebnis* (result), *Kenntnis* (knowledge), and *Erlebnis* (experience). Nouns ending in -nis are usually neuter, though a handful are feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Ich habe volles Verständnis für deine Situation.
words.exampleTranslation: I have full understanding for your situation.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms agent nouns -- the person or thing that does the action. Lehrer (teacher), Fahrer (driver), Baecker (baker). One of the most productive suffixes in German.

This root comes from the verb *fahren* (to travel, to drive, to ride) and shows up across a huge family of everyday words. You'll spot it in *Erfahrung* (experience, literally a journeying-through), *Gefahr* (danger, from an old sense of what you encounter on a journey), *Fahrzeug* (vehicle), and *Bahnhof* (well, paired with *fahren* in countless compound signs). Recognizing it helps unlock a surprisingly wide cluster of common vocabulary.

The most common German noun-forming suffix. Turns a verb into a noun for the action, its process, or its result. Verwandlung (transformation, from verwandeln), Bedeutung (meaning, from bedeuten). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Sie hat viel Erfahrung im Umgang mit Kindern.
words.exampleTranslation: She has a lot of experience working with children.

parts.homeLangDetails: Turns intransitive verbs into transitive ones, or focuses the action on a specific object. Think of it as making the verb reach out and grab something -- besuchen (to visit) vs suchen (to search), bedecken (to cover) vs decken (to lay).

The most common German noun-forming suffix. Turns a verb into a noun for the action, its process, or its result. Verwandlung (transformation, from verwandeln), Bedeutung (meaning, from bedeuten). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Die Beschreibung des Täters half der Polizei bei der Suche.
words.exampleTranslation: The description of the perpetrator helped the police in their search.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ent- signals separation or the cutting away of ambiguity — making a clear break between options. The underlying idea is of resolving something decisively, splitting apart what was undecided.

From scheiden (to separate, to part, to divide). Think of cutting through uncertainty — the same root appears in Scheidung (divorce, literally a separation) and unterscheiden (to distinguish, to differentiate).

The most common German noun-forming suffix. Turns a verb into a noun for the action, its process, or its result. Verwandlung (transformation, from verwandeln), Bedeutung (meaning, from bedeuten). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Die Entscheidung war nicht leicht, aber notwendig.
words.exampleTranslation: The decision was not easy, but necessary.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- signals a thorough transformation — not just changing something slightly, but altering it in a fundamental way. Compare verändern (to change/alter) vs. ändern (to modify slightly).
From ändern (to change, to alter). The same root appears in anders (different) and anderer (other). This is the core verb being nominalized here.

The most common German noun-forming suffix. Turns a verb into a noun for the action, its process, or its result. Verwandlung (transformation, from verwandeln), Bedeutung (meaning, from bedeuten). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Die Veränderung im Wetter war sehr plötzlich.
words.exampleTranslation: The change in the weather was very sudden.

parts.homeLangDetails: Turns intransitive verbs into transitive ones, or focuses the action on a specific object. Think of it as making the verb reach out and grab something -- besuchen (to visit) vs suchen (to search), bedecken (to cover) vs decken (to lay).

The most common German noun-forming suffix. Turns a verb into a noun for the action, its process, or its result. Verwandlung (transformation, from verwandeln), Bedeutung (meaning, from bedeuten). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Die Besprechung beginnt um neun Uhr.
words.exampleTranslation: The meeting begins at nine o'clock.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here über- carries the sense of 'across' — carrying meaning from one language over to another. The same prefix in übersetzen can also mean 'to ferry across' water, and the overlap is not accidental.
From setzen (to set, to place). Think of it as 'placing words across' from one language into another. The same root appears in einsetzen (to deploy) and umsetzen (to implement/convert).

The most common German noun-forming suffix. Turns a verb into a noun for the action, its process, or its result. Verwandlung (transformation, from verwandeln), Bedeutung (meaning, from bedeuten). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Die Übersetzung des Romans dauerte zwei Jahre.
words.exampleTranslation: The translation of the novel took two years.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here be- turns the noun Hand (hand) into a verb — behandeln literally means 'to hand-le' something, to deal with or attend to it. The prefix directs the action onto an object.

This is -lung, the contracted form of -ung after a stem ending in -l (behandeln → Behandlung). It functions identically to -ung, turning the verb into a noun meaning the act or result of treating/handling.

words.exampleSentence: Die Behandlung im Krankenhaus war sehr gut.
words.exampleTranslation: The treatment at the hospital was very good.

parts.homeLangDetails: From lösen (to loosen, to dissolve, to solve). The idea is of untying a knot — whether a physical object or an abstract problem. Related to los (loose, free) and English 'loose' and 'lose'.

The most common German noun-forming suffix. Turns a verb into a noun for the action, its process, or its result. Verwandlung (transformation, from verwandeln), Bedeutung (meaning, from bedeuten). Always feminine.

words.exampleSentence: Wir haben endlich eine Lösung gefunden.
words.exampleTranslation: We have finally found a solution.

words.exampleSentence: Vergiss nicht, deinen Handschuh mitzunehmen — es ist kalt draußen.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget to take your glove — it's cold outside.

words.exampleSentence: Dieses Kunstwerk verkörpert den Zeitgeist der 1920er Jahre.
words.exampleTranslation: This artwork embodies the spirit of the 1920s.

words.exampleSentence: Wir müssen um sechs Uhr am Flughafen sein.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to be at the airport at six o'clock.

words.exampleSentence: Der Zug fährt in zehn Minuten vom Bahnhof ab.
words.exampleTranslation: The train departs from the station in ten minutes.

parts.homeLangDetails: From krank (sick, ill). The -en here is a linking element connecting the adjective stem to the compound, not a verb or plural ending. The root krank is closely related to English 'cringe' and appears in words like Krankheit (illness) and Krankenwagen (ambulance).

words.exampleSentence: Er wurde sofort ins Krankenhaus gebracht.
words.exampleTranslation: He was taken to the hospital immediately.

words.exampleSentence: Bitte stell die Milch zurück in den Kühlschrank.
words.exampleTranslation: Please put the milk back in the refrigerator.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms agent nouns -- the person or thing that does the action. Lehrer (teacher), Fahrer (driver), Baecker (baker). One of the most productive suffixes in German.

words.exampleSentence: Der Fernseher im Wohnzimmer ist kaputt.
words.exampleTranslation: The television in the living room is broken.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. Like -s-, it carries no independent meaning and exists purely to smooth the join between morphemes.

words.exampleSentence: Der Herbst ist meine liebste Jahreszeit.
words.exampleTranslation: Autumn is my favorite season.

words.exampleSentence: Meine Tochter geht seit September in den Kindergarten.
words.exampleTranslation: My daughter has been going to kindergarten since September.

words.exampleSentence: Meine Bücher liegen auf dem Schreibtisch.
words.exampleTranslation: My books are on the desk.

words.exampleSentence: Ich habe heute einen Brief im Briefkasten gefunden.
words.exampleTranslation: I found a letter in the mailbox today.

words.exampleSentence: Wir sitzen abends immer im Wohnzimmer zusammen.
words.exampleTranslation: We always sit together in the living room in the evenings.

parts.homeLangDetails: This root comes from the verb *fahren* (to travel, to drive, to ride) and shows up across a huge family of everyday words. You'll spot it in *Erfahrung* (experience, literally a journeying-through), *Gefahr* (danger, from an old sense of what you encounter on a journey), *Fahrzeug* (vehicle), and *Bahnhof* (well, paired with *fahren* in countless compound signs). Recognizing it helps unlock a surprisingly wide cluster of common vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: Bitte zeigen Sie Ihre Fahrkarte beim Einsteigen.
words.exampleTranslation: Please show your ticket when boarding.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms agent nouns -- the person or thing that does the action. Lehrer (teacher), Fahrer (driver), Baecker (baker). One of the most productive suffixes in German.

words.exampleSentence: Sie kaufte einen Reiseführer über Italien.
words.exampleTranslation: She bought a travel guide about Italy.

parts.homeLangDetails: Self. Appears in compound nouns and adjectives where the subject acts on or refers to itself. Selbstvertrauen (self-confidence), selbststaendig (independent, self-standing).

Here ver- intensifies the sense of trusting into a complete, established state — you've fully entrusted yourself to yourself. It's closer to a sense of groundedness or reliance than something going away.
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.exampleSentence: Sie hat viel Selbstvertrauen in ihrer Arbeit.
words.exampleTranslation: She has a lot of self-confidence in her work.

parts.homeLangDetails: Together with or accompanying. Mitbringen (to bring along), mitdenken (to think along with someone, to follow their reasoning).
One of the most versatile prefixes in German. It marks past participles (gemacht, done), forms collective nouns (Gebirge, mountain range, from Berg), and gives a sense of things coming together.

words.exampleSentence: Er zeigte großes Mitgefühl für seine Freunde.
words.exampleTranslation: He showed great compassion for his friends.

parts.homeLangDetails: Against or in opposition to something. Widerstand (resistance, a standing-against), widersprechen (to contradict, to speak against).
From sprechen (to speak), here in its noun form Spruch — a spoken declaration or utterance. So Widerspruch is literally 'a speaking-against,' which is exactly what a contradiction or objection is.

words.exampleSentence: Es gibt einen offensichtlichen Widerspruch in seiner Argumentation.
words.exampleTranslation: There is an obvious contradiction in his argument.

parts.homeLangDetails: Under or below. Can suggest support from below or being subordinate. Unterstuetzen (to support, to prop up from underneath), unterstreichen (to underline).
A Fugenlaut variant inserted between compound parts. It looks like a plural ending but here carries no grammatical meaning -- it just connects the parts.

words.exampleSentence: Das Unternehmen wurde vor zwanzig Jahren gegründet.
words.exampleTranslation: The company was founded twenty years ago.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here gegen doesn't mean 'against' in the adversarial sense but rather 'facing' or 'in front of' — what is directly before you right now. It gives the word its sense of immediate presence.
From warten (to wait) or the older sense of 'being present, attending.' The root conveys being at a place or moment — so Gegenwart is what is present before you, i.e., the present moment.

words.translationLong: the present, the present time, presence
words.exampleSentence: Es ist wichtig, in der Gegenwart zu leben.
words.exampleTranslation: It is important to live in the present.

parts.homeLangDetails: Turns nouns or verbs into adjectives meaning like or characteristic of. Freundlich (friendly), taeglich (daily), moeglich (possible). Often matches English -ly or -like.

words.exampleSentence: Sie ist immer sehr freundlich zu ihren Nachbarn.
words.exampleTranslation: She is always very friendly to her neighbors.

parts.homeLangDetails: Turns intransitive verbs into transitive ones, or focuses the action on a specific object. Think of it as making the verb reach out and grab something -- besuchen (to visit) vs suchen (to search), bedecken (to cover) vs decken (to lay).
Turns nouns or verbs into adjectives meaning like or characteristic of. Freundlich (friendly), taeglich (daily), moeglich (possible). Often matches English -ly or -like.

words.exampleSentence: Kann ich Ihnen behilflich sein?
words.exampleTranslation: Can I be of help to you?

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- doesn't carry a sense of going astray or away, but rather of formal, complete enactment — being answerable in a fully binding sense.
Turns nouns or verbs into adjectives meaning like or characteristic of. Freundlich (friendly), taeglich (daily), moeglich (possible). Often matches English -ly or -like.

words.exampleSentence: Wer ist für diesen Fehler verantwortlich?
words.exampleTranslation: Who is responsible for this mistake?

parts.homeLangDetails: The straightforward negating prefix, like English un-. Turns the meaning of the base word on its head. Found in hundreds of German words: unglaublich (unbelievable), Unruhe (unrest).
Turns nouns or verbs into adjectives meaning like or characteristic of. Freundlich (friendly), taeglich (daily), moeglich (possible). Often matches English -ly or -like.

words.exampleSentence: Die Aussicht vom Gipfel war unglaublich schön.
words.exampleTranslation: The view from the summit was incredibly beautiful.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms agent nouns -- the person or thing that does the action. Lehrer (teacher), Fahrer (driver), Baecker (baker). One of the most productive suffixes in German.

From erhalten (to receive, to obtain) — the stem halten means 'to hold' but in combination with er- it takes on the sense of receiving or obtaining something. Think of it as 'bringing something into your hold.'
Here -lich forms an adjective meaning '-able' — obtainable, available. It functions more like '-able' than '-like' in this context.

words.exampleSentence: Das Produkt ist in allen Apotheken erhältlich.
words.exampleTranslation: The product is available in all pharmacies.

parts.homeLangDetails: From würdig (worthy) or Würde (dignity, worth). Something merkwürdig is literally 'worth noticing' — and by extension, it's become the standard word for 'strange' or 'peculiar,' because things that stand out demand your attention.
Forms adjectives meaning having the quality of or characterized by. Hungrig (hungry), wichtig (important, weighty), maechtig (powerful, mighty).

words.exampleSentence: Er benahm sich heute sehr merkwürdig.
words.exampleTranslation: He behaved very strangely today.

parts.homeLangDetails: Points outward, away from a source, or to full completion. Ausgehen is to go out; ausschlafen is to sleep all the way through until fully rested.
From führen (to lead, to carry out). The same root appears in Führer (leader), durchführen (to carry through), and einführen (to introduce). Here it suggests 'carrying out' detail.
Turns nouns or verbs into adjectives meaning like or characteristic of. Freundlich (friendly), taeglich (daily), moeglich (possible). Often matches English -ly or -like.

words.exampleSentence: Er hat die Situation ausführlich erklärt.
words.exampleTranslation: He explained the situation in detail.

parts.homeLangDetails: Self. Appears in compound nouns and adjectives where the subject acts on or refers to itself. Selbstvertrauen (self-confidence), selbststaendig (independent, self-standing).
From stehen/Stand (to stand, standing position). Think of 'standing on one's own.' The same root appears in Zustand (condition, state) and Bestand (stock, inventory).
Forms adjectives meaning having the quality of or characterized by. Hungrig (hungry), wichtig (important, weighty), maechtig (powerful, mighty).

words.exampleSentence: Sie arbeitet selbstständig als Grafikdesignerin.
words.exampleTranslation: She works independently as a graphic designer.

parts.homeLangDetails: Something happening before, in front of, or in advance. Vorbereiten (to prepare, to ready something beforehand), vorstellen (to introduce, to place before someone).
From sehen (to see); Sicht means 'sight' or 'view.' Vorsicht literally means 'foresight' — looking ahead — hence the common warning 'Vorsicht!' (Watch out!).
Forms adjectives meaning having the quality of or characterized by. Hungrig (hungry), wichtig (important, weighty), maechtig (powerful, mighty).

words.exampleSentence: Sei vorsichtig auf der nassen Straße!
words.exampleTranslation: Be careful on the wet road!

parts.homeLangDetails: Gleich means 'equal,' 'same,' or 'even.' It appears in Gleichgewicht (balance), gleichzeitig (simultaneous), and Gleichheit (equality).
From Maß (measure). Combined with gleich, it conveys the idea of 'equal measure' — even, regular, consistent. The same root appears in mäßig (moderate) and Maßstab (scale, standard).
Forms adjectives meaning having the quality of or characterized by. Hungrig (hungry), wichtig (important, weighty), maechtig (powerful, mighty).

words.exampleSentence: Der Motor läuft gleichmäßig.
words.exampleTranslation: The engine runs smoothly.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Fugenlaut, a connective sound inserted between compound parts for ease of pronunciation. It carries no meaning of its own and is one of the most common linking elements in German compounds.
The German suffix for without, matching English -less. Sinnlos (senseless), hoffnungslos (hopeless), grenzenlos (limitless).

words.exampleSentence: Er ist seit drei Monaten arbeitslos.
words.exampleTranslation: He has been unemployed for three months.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Fugenlaut, a connective sound inserted between compound parts for ease of pronunciation. It carries no meaning of its own and is one of the most common linking elements in German compounds.
The German suffix for without, matching English -less. Sinnlos (senseless), hoffnungslos (hopeless), grenzenlos (limitless).

words.exampleSentence: Die Lage schien hoffnungslos.
words.exampleTranslation: The situation seemed hopeless.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Sprache (language, speech). The root sprach is the past-tense stem of sprechen (to speak), but here it functions as the noun Sprache in compound form. Being sprachlos is literally being without speech — struck speechless.
The German suffix for without, matching English -less. Sinnlos (senseless), hoffnungslos (hopeless), grenzenlos (limitless).

words.translationLong: speechless, at a loss for words
words.exampleSentence: Ich war einfach sprachlos, als ich die Nachricht hörte.
words.exampleTranslation: I was simply speechless when I heard the news.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Hilfe (help, aid). The compound uses the verb stem hilf- rather than the full noun form. The same root appears in helfen (to help) and hilfreich (helpful).
The German suffix for without, matching English -less. Sinnlos (senseless), hoffnungslos (hopeless), grenzenlos (limitless).

words.exampleSentence: Das kleine Kind stand hilflos auf der Straße.
words.exampleTranslation: The small child stood helplessly on the street.

parts.homeLangDetails: Sinn covers a range of meanings: sense, meaning, purpose, and even 'mind.' Sinnlos therefore can mean senseless, pointless, or meaningless depending on context. The same root appears in sinnvoll (meaningful) and Unsinn (nonsense).
The German suffix for without, matching English -less. Sinnlos (senseless), hoffnungslos (hopeless), grenzenlos (limitless).

words.exampleSentence: Es ist sinnlos, jetzt noch zu streiten.
words.exampleTranslation: It is pointless to argue now.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms agent nouns -- the person or thing that does the action. Lehrer (teacher), Fahrer (driver), Baecker (baker). One of the most productive suffixes in German.

From klar (clear) and klären (to clarify, to clear up). Erklären literally means 'to make clear' or 'to clarify completely.' The same root appears in Kläranlage (water treatment plant) and aufklären (to enlighten).
Signals that something can be done, matching English -able or -ible. Machbar (doable), erkennbar (recognizable), verwendbar (usable).

words.exampleSentence: Das Phänomen ist wissenschaftlich erklärbar.
words.exampleTranslation: The phenomenon is scientifically explainable.

parts.homeLangDetails: From lösen (to solve, to loosen, to dissolve). The root carries the sense of releasing or untying something — a problem 'solved' is one untied or released. The same root appears in Lösung (solution) and auflösen (to dissolve, to disband).
Signals that something can be done, matching English -able or -ible. Machbar (doable), erkennbar (recognizable), verwendbar (usable).

words.exampleSentence: Das Problem ist lösbar, wenn wir zusammenarbeiten.
words.exampleTranslation: The problem is solvable if we work together.

parts.homeLangDetails: From tragen (to carry, to bear, to wear). Tragbar can mean physically portable (carry-able) or figuratively tolerable (bear-able). The same root appears in Vertrag (contract) and ertragen (to endure).
Signals that something can be done, matching English -able or -ible. Machbar (doable), erkennbar (recognizable), verwendbar (usable).

words.exampleSentence: Dieser Laptop ist leicht und tragbar.
words.exampleTranslation: This laptop is light and portable.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- signals transformation — changing something into a different state. This is one of ver-'s common roles, as in verwandeln (to transform) and verbessern (to improve). It turns ändern (to alter) into verändern (to change fundamentally).
From ändern (to alter, to change). Related to 'ander' (other) — to change something is to make it 'other.' The same root appears in anders (differently) and Änderung (alteration).
Signals that something can be done, matching English -able or -ible. Machbar (doable), erkennbar (recognizable), verwendbar (usable).

words.exampleSentence: Die Einstellungen sind jederzeit veränderbar.
words.exampleTranslation: The settings can be changed at any time.

parts.homeLangDetails: The straightforward negating prefix, like English un-. Turns the meaning of the base word on its head. Found in hundreds of German words: unglaublich (unbelievable), Unruhe (unrest).

From brauchen (to need, to use) and Brauch (custom, use). Brauchbar means usable or useful; unbrauchbar flips that to unusable or useless. The same root appears in gebrauchen (to make use of) and Gebrauch (use, usage).
Signals that something can be done, matching English -able or -ible. Machbar (doable), erkennbar (recognizable), verwendbar (usable).

words.exampleSentence: Das alte Gerät ist völlig unbrauchbar.
words.exampleTranslation: The old device is completely unusable.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Friede(n) (peace). This root is ancient and appears across many German names like Friedrich and Siegfried, as well as Friedhof (cemetery, literally 'peace courtyard') and friedfertig (peaceable).
Turns nouns or verbs into adjectives meaning like or characteristic of. Freundlich (friendly), taeglich (daily), moeglich (possible). Often matches English -ly or -like.

words.exampleSentence: Die Demonstration verlief friedlich.
words.exampleTranslation: The demonstration proceeded peacefully.
